coredata.dc:title: Efficient green synthesis of N,B co-doped bright fluorescent carbon nanodots and their electrocatalytic and bio-imaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Arul, Velusamy
Chandrasekaran, Pitchai
Sivaraman, Gandhi
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon nanodots (NB-CNDs) were synthesized from the Solanum betaceum (S. betaceum) fruit extract viz. a simple hydrothermal carbonization method. The as-prepared NB-CNDs were characterized by regular spectroanalytical techniques. The nitrogen and boron co-doping on the surface of carbon nanodots were confirmed by FT-IR, EDAX and XPS studies. The optical properties of the NB-CNDs were studied using UV–Vis and Fluorescence spectroscopy. The quantum yield (QY) for the synthesized NB-CNDs was found to be 35%. The HR-TEM results revealed that the NB-CNDs were spherical in shape and the average size was 5 nm. The XRD, Raman and SAED studies were confirmed the amorphous nature of the NB-CNDs. The electrocatalytic activity of NB-CNDs on oxidation of hydrazine hydrate (HH) was studied by cyclic voltammetric techniques. The NB-CNDs were efficiently utilized as an electrocatalyst for the hydrazine hydrate oxidation in the potential range between −0.4 V to 1 V with the scan rate of 50 mV/s. Further, the synthesized fluorescent NB-CNDs were used as an effective fluorescent staining agent for the in-vitro bio-imaging studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solanum betaceum
Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon nanodots
Electrocatalytic ability
Oxidation
Hydrazine hydrate
In-vitro bio-imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image at 20 nm scale bar (b) size chart of NB-CNDs (c) HR-TEM image at 5 nm scale bar and (d) SAED pattern for NB-CNDs.
EDAX spectrum of synthesized NB-CNDs obtained from Solanum betaceum fruit extract.
X-ray Diffraction pattern of NB-CNDs and undoped carbon dots obtained from Solanum betaceum fruit extract.
Raman spectrum for synthesized NB-CNDs and shows D and G bands.
(a) Full survey XP spectrum of the prepared NB-CNDs; deconvoluted XP spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s and (e) B1s.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra for S. betaceum precursor and prepared NB-CNDs and (b) corresponding excitation spectrum of NB-CNDs.
Fluorescence spectra of NB-CNDs at the different excitation wavelength (a) from 270 nm to 310 nm and (b) from 310 nm to 400 nm.
Cyclic voltammogramic responses for bare and NB-CNDs modified GC electrode in 0.1 M PBS.

a) Cyclic Voltammogramic responses for the bare GC and NB-CNDs modified GC in HH/PBS solution b) Cyclic Voltammogramic responses for the NB-CNDs modified GC in oxidation of HH using NB-CNDs modified GC electrode in 0.1 M PBS.
Concentration Vs current plot for the oxidation of HH using NB-CNDs modified GC electrode in 0.1 M PBS.
Cytotoxicity plot for HeLa cells after 24 h incubation with NB-CNDs (5.0 mg/mL).
Confocal microscopy image of HeLa Cells incubated with NB-CNDs (5.0 mg/mL) (a) bright field; (b) excitation at 410 nm (blue); (c) excitation at 480 nm (green); and (d) excitation at 580 nm (red).
Comparison of reported QY values of the doped CDs with synthesized NB-CNDs.

head.ce:title: Efficient green synthesis of N,B co-doped bright fluorescent carbon nanodots and their electrocatalytic and bio-imaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram - 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram - 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon nanodots (NB-CNDs) were synthesized from the Solanum betaceum (S. betaceum) fruit extract viz. a simple hydrothermal carbonization method. The as-prepared NB-CNDs were characterized by regular spectroanalytical techniques. The nitrogen and boron co-doping on the surface of carbon nanodots were confirmed by FT-IR, EDAX and XPS studies. The optical properties of the NB-CNDs were studied using UV–Vis and Fluorescence spectroscopy. The quantum yield (QY) for the synthesized NB-CNDs was found to be 35%. The HR-TEM results revealed that the NB-CNDs were spherical in shape and the average size was 5 nm. The XRD, Raman and SAED studies were confirmed the amorphous nature of the NB-CNDs. The electrocatalytic activity of NB-CNDs on oxidation of hydrazine hydrate (HH) was studied by cyclic voltammetric techniques. The NB-CNDs were efficiently utilized as an electrocatalyst for the hydrazine hydrate oxidation in the potential range between −0.4 V to 1 V with the scan rate of 50 mV/s. Further, the synthesized fluorescent NB-CNDs were used as an effective fluorescent staining agent for the in-vitro bio-imaging studies.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon nanodots

ce:section.ce:para: “Carbon nanodots” (CNDs) are carbon based highly fluorescent nanomaterials which belong to the family of semiconductor quantum dots (SQCDs) with size between 1 to 10 nm in diameter [1,2]. CNDs gained prominence over the traditional SQCDs due to their outstanding advantages such as easy synthesis, small size, superior aqueous solubility, least toxicity, good chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, easy functionalization and eco-friendly nature [3,4]. CNDs have been used in wide-range of potential applications such as catalysis, bio-imaging, medical diagnosis, sensors and photovoltaic devices [5–8].

Doping is the ideal process of adding elements (or) metals on the surface of the CNDs to tailor the electrical and fluorescent properties, defects and functional groups [9,10]. The catalytic ability and photoluminescence quantum yield are low for native CNDs compared to doped CNDs. Hence, the doping process is necessary for improving the photophysical properties and consequently their utilities in various applications. Doping of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, boron, sulphur, and phosphorous over the CNDs surface would improve their fluorescent properties drastically [10–14]. There are quite a number of researches available for single heteroatom doping especially Nitrogen as dopant [15–17]. In fact, nitrogen doping or co-doping of nitrogen with B (or) S (or) P significantly enhances the fluorescent quantum yield [18,19]. Heteroatom doping could alter the valence/conduction band positions on the carbon materials which results in the modification of properties [20]. Hence, the co-doped CNDs have become efficient candidates for catalytic, biological and sensing applications. Li et al. had reported that the N and S doped CDs could be used as the photocatalyst for the methylene blue degradation and fluorescent probe for algal imaging [21]. Recently Yushan liu et al. have synthesized the N,B co-doped CDs and discussed the sensing and multicolor bio-imaging [22] and Qianghua Ye et al. reported N and B co-doped CDs as the efficient sensing and imaging probe for human umbilical vein endothelial cells [23]. In this work we have doped the N and B on CNDs. The boric acid is the most widely used and low cost source for boron doping. Boron formed covalent bond with CDs and especially B doping introduces P-type carriers which results efficiently modifying the electronic and optical properties of the carbon matrix and utilized them as a potential candidate for bio-imaging and bio-sensing applications. Moreover, the N and B doping remarkably enhanced quantum yield (QY) of the CNDs. [23,24]

Hydrazine hydrate (HH) is toxic to environment and also a carcinogenic. HH is a strong reducing agent and widely used in explosive industries, rocket fuel manufacturing, catalysis and pharmaceuticals [25–27]. The oxidation of HH is an essential process in the fields of fuel cells (electrocatalysis) and electrochemical sensors [27,28]. For electrochemical oxidation of HH, glassy carbon electrode (GC) could be used instead of conventional bare electrodes for avoiding high over potential caused by slow electron transfer kinetics [29].

S. betaceum commonly known as Tamarillo is a good source of Vitamins A, C, E and B6 apart from calcium, iron and Potassium [30,31]. Tamarillo fruits are also used as antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory agents for treatment of sore throats. The anti-oxidant and anticancer properties of the fruits are reported [32,33]. This renewable and highly abundant bio resource was used as a carbon source for the synthesis of CNDs because of its sustainability, nominal cost [34].

In the present study, S. betaceum (tamarillo) fruit extract, aqueous ammonia and boric acid were used as a carbon precursor, nitrogen and boron source, respectively for the synthesis of nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (NB-CNDs) by simple hydrothermal carbonization method. The synthesized NB-CNDs were characterized using UV, PL, TEM, FT-IR, XRD, Raman, XPS and NMR studies. The electrocatalytic ability of the synthesized NB-CNDs was examined through the oxidation of Hydrazine hydrate (HH) by glassy carbon electrode (GC) using cyclic voltammetry. Moreover, the synthesized NB-CNDs were investigated for the possible use as an effective fluorescent staining agent in the in-vitro bio-imaging studies.

The NB-CNDs were prepared by simple hydrothermal method using the fruit extract and particular precursor for doping. When the extract was subjected to hydrothermal carbonization, the color of the S. betaceum fruit extract was changed from red to blackish brown which initially confirmed the formation of carbon dots [35]. The obtained final filtrate containing the fluorescent NB-CNDs was carefully kept in a refrigerator at below 10 °C for further use.

HR-TEM analysis provides the surface morphology, size, crystalline nature and elemental details of the NB-CNDs. Fig. 1 (a) and (c) illustrates the HR-TEM images of the NB-CNDs, which are finely dispersed in water with size distribution within 0 to 10 nm (Fig. 1(b)). The average particle size of NB-CNDs was 5 nm and they were found to be spherical in shape. The amorphous nature of the carbon dots was confirmed from non-dotted clear circles of SAED pattern (Fig. 1(d)) [16,26,27]. HR-TEM image of the synthesized undoped carbon dots shows the spherical in nature and evenly distributed (ESM 1).The average particle size of the as-synthesized undoped carbon dots was calculated to be 5.7 nm.

The Fig. 2 displays the energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) of NB-CNDs and it could provide the elemental composition of the synthesized NB-CNDs. The NB-CNDs consisted mainly of C (72.10%), O (15.81%), N (7.45%), and B (5.54%) which confirmed that the surfaces of the NB-CNDs were functionalized with nitrogen and boron atoms [36,37].

The surface of the NB-CNDs and its functional groups were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. ESM 2 shows the FT-IR spectra of the extract, undoped carbon dots and synthesized NB-CNDs. The spectrum of S. betaceum extract (pink line) shows intense wide absorption bands at 3425, 2922, 1622 and 1042 cm−1 due to the presence of –OH, –CH, CO and C–O–C functional groups, respectively. The spectrum of NB-CNDs (blue line) showed bands at 3432, 2922, and 1667 cm−1 which ascribed to –OH/–NH, –CH, and –C=O, respectively [37,51–53]. The –C=C stretching at 1595 cm−1 indicates the sp2 hybridized honeycomb lattice present in the CNDs core [56]. The B-O-C str band at 1104 cm−1 indicated that the boron atom was doped on the CNDs [55]. The intense bands at 3432 and 1408 cm−1 belong to the -N-H and CN stretching which revealed that the doping of nitrogen over the CDs [4,56]. The results of the FT-IR spectra thus confirmed the presence of acid, hydroxyl, amine and carbonyl moieties in the prepared NB-CNDs. For the comparison, the FT-IR spectrum of undoped carbon dots (black line) shows the functional groups –OH, CH, CO, and CC groups present over the carbon dots. XRD spectroscopy was employed to find out degree of crystallinity, phase and structure of the synthesized NB-CNDs and undoped carbon dots [54]. Fig. 3 provides the XRD pattern for the synthesized NB-CNDs. The (002) plane and (100) planes of graphitic carbon of the NB-CNDs were evident from the wide peaks at 2θ = 24.3° and 2θ = 42.2° respectively. In general, XRD peak for C (002) plane of graphitic carbon shows at 2θ = 26°.

In general, XRD peak for C (002) plane of graphitic carbon shows at 2θ = 26°. In this present study, the C (002) plane appeared at 2θ = 24.3° which possess the modest amorphous nature due to the functionalization of boron and nitrogen atoms in CDs. In addition to that, the peaks at 26°, 32°, and 40° were attributed to the potassium impurities which come from the carbon source Solanum betaceum. The as-synthesized undoped carbon dots diffraction C (002) plane shows at 2θ = 22.4° which confirms the amorphous nature of undoped carbon dots. In undoped carbon dots, the potassium impurities peak at 29°, 34°, 38°, and 44° which arise from the carbon source. Moreover, the XRD results clearly revealed that the synthesized NB-CNDs and undoped carbon dots have moderate graphitic structure [57,58].

The broad XRD peak appeared perhaps to the presence of small sized NB-CNDs. The calculated d-spacing value of synthesized NB-CNDs as per Bragg's equation were 0.37 nm and 0.21 nm which belong to the C (002) plane and C (100) plane. The d-spacing (interlayer distance) values corroborated with the HR-TEM results for synthesized NB-CNDs. Using the Debye-Scherer Eq. (1), the average size of the undoped carbon dots and NB-CNDs were calculated to be ~5.7 and 5 nm.
(1)
B
2θ
= Kλ / L
cos
θ
where B is the line broadening at half the maximum intensity (FWHM) in radians, K is the dimensionless shape factor, typically the value of it is nearer to unity, l is the incident X-ray wavelength in nanometer (0.154 nm), L is the average size of the crystalline domains [7].

Fig. 4 displays Raman spectrum of NB-CNDs. The Raman spectra demonstrates the two divergent bands at 1353 cm−1 and 1601 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G bands which indicate the sp3 defects in the graphitic carbon and vibrations occurred in the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, respectively [59]. The D and G band intensities are indicated as I
D and I
G and the ratio between these intensities could be less than 1 for ideal graphite. The I
D and I
G values were found to be 0.19 for NB-CNDs and the intensity of I
D is less than the intensity of I
G (I
D < I
G ). The Raman results demonstrated the moderate graphitic structure of the synthesized NB-CNDs and this could also reveal that the minimum quantity of carbonaceous impurities had been present during the synthesis of NB-CNDs [56].

The elemental details of the NB-CNDs and its accurate mass could be obtained from X-ray Photo Electron Spectroscopy (XPS) technique. Fig. 5 depicts the full survey XPS spectrum of the synthesized NB-CNDs and its deconvoluted XPS spectra for the elements of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), oxygen (O1s) and boron (B1s). In the full survey scan spectrum (Fig. 5(a)) four distinct peaks appeared at 284.6, 398.2, 529.6 and 188.7 eV, for elemental carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and boron, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 5(b)) showed two major peaks at 282.6, and 284.7, which were attributed to CH, and CC (sp2), respectively [37,60,61]. Moreover two minor peaks appear at 290.5 and 293.2 eV which confirms the presence of potassium impurities.

Especially, one of the C1s peaks at 284.7 eV revealed that the synthesized NB-CNDs mainly consist of sp2 carbon atoms [56]. Next, Fig. 5(c) depicts the deconvoluted N1s spectrum which shows three peaks at 396.9, 398.02 and 398.9 eV which were attributed to amide (O=C-NH2, amine (C-NH2)) and pyridinic N groups present over the surface of the NB-CNDs, respectively [16,62,63]. Fig. 5(d) provides the deconvoluted O1s spectrum which shows two peaks at 528.7 and 529.9 eV which attributed to C-O-C and -C-O/-C-N-O functional groups, respectively [16,17]. Finally, Fig. 5(e) displays the deconvoluted B1s spectrum which shows four peaks at 189.5, 190.6, 195.8 and 197.8 eV which were related to BN2C, BC, BC2O and –BCO2 groups, respectively [64,65]. The XPS results thus clearly showed that the presence of C, N, O and B in the synthesized NB-CNDs and their atomic percentages (%) of C, O, N and B were 72.16, 15.75, 7.50 and 5.49, respectively. The results of the study strongly support that the nitrogen and boron were doped over the CDs surface. The obtained XPS results thus corroborate to the results of FT-IR spectroscopy and EDS analysis. The full survey spectrum and deconvoluted spectrum of the as-synthesized undoped carbon dots shown in ESM 3. The deconvoluted spectrum of C1s shows the two major peaks at 284.7 and 286.3 eV which related to CC and CO moieties, respectively. Moreover one minor peak shows at 288.7 eV which correlated to the impurities of the potassium which arise from the biogenic source of the S. betaceum. The deconvoluted spectrum of O1s shows two major peaks at 532.3 and 533 eV which correlated to C-OH/C-O-C and CO groups, respectively [53,55].

Fig. ESM 4 and ESM 5 depict the 1H and 13C NMR spectrum of the synthesized NB-CNDs. In 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. ESM 2) CH protons (sp3) were found in the region of 1 to –3 ppm. The protons attached with ether, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups were found in the region 3 to –6 ppm. The signals between 6 to 8 ppm indicated the presence of aromatic protons [66,67]. The signal at 8.4 ppm could be attributed to aldehydic protons [68].

The 13C spectra of NB-CNDs (Fig. ESM 3) revealed that there were three different kinds of regions as shown in Fig. S3. The region between 20 to 100 ppm indicates the CO and C-OH groups (sp3 carbons). The signals between 100 to 150 ppm arise due to the sp2 carbon atoms (C=C) and the region between 160 to 180 ppm indicated the presence of CO groups (sp2 carbon) in the synthesized NB-CNDs [65,67]. The results of 1H and 13C NMR spectra clearly revealed the presence of –OH, -C-O-C and –COOH groups in the surface of the synthesized NB-CNDs which were responsible for superior aqueous solubility [66].

Fig. 6 (a) depicts the UV–Vis spectral graph for the aqueous extract of S. betaceum precursor and prepared NB-CNDs. UV–Vis spectrum of S. betaceum extract (black line) shows peak at 267 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of the polyphenols present in the extract. The UV spectrum of NB-CNDs (Red line) showed two peaks at 271 nm and 305 nm, which correspond to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of CC (sp2) graphitic carbon and the non-bonding electrons of the nitrogen or carbonyl groups, respectively [37–39]. These results were confirmed by the fluorescence excitation spectrum of NB-CNDs (Fig. 6(b) showed a peak at 310 nm). UV–Vis and fluorescence excitation spectra of undoped carbon dots is shown in ESM 6. In ESM 6 (a) the undoped carbon dots shows one peak at 268 nm which corresponds to π-π* transitions of CC (sp2) graphitic carbon. The fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized NB-CNDs are depicted in Fig. 7 with varying the excitation wavelengths 270 nm to 400 nm.

The fluorescent intensity of the NB-CNDs was increased gradually in Fig. 7(a) due to varying of the excitation wavelength from 270 to 310 nm. This kind of transformation occurred in emission spectra due to π-π* transitions of graphitic carbon cores.

Next, in Fig. 7(b) there was a steady decrease observed in the fluorescent intensity while increasing the excitation wavelength from 310 to 400 nm which could be ascribed to n-π* transition because of the nitrogen moieties containing non-bonding electrons. The maximum emission intensity (λmax) of 410 nm was noted in the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The fluorescence spectra of the undoped carbon dots are depicted in ESM.7 with varying the excitation wavelengths from 270 nm to 400 nm.

The quantum yield (QY) for the synthesized NB-CNDs was found to be 35% (quinine sulphate as reference). The QY of the various heteroatoms doped carbon dots QY were compared with the synthesized NB-CNDs (Table 1 ).

This type of excitation dependant emission behaviour of the synthesized NB-CNDs arises because of the varying sizes of CDs on the NB-CNDs surface [49,50]. Due to this remarkable fluorescence property, the synthesized NB-CNDs have been widely used in sensing and biological applications [51].

Electrocatalytic ability of the NB-CNDs was examined through oxidation of hydrazine hydrate (HH). Fig. 8 shows the cyclic voltammogramic responses for bare glassy carbon (GC) electrode and modified GC in 0.1 M PBS solution. In bare GC voltammogramic response, no considerable response was observed. But in the case of modified GC, the response was entirely different from that of bare GC. This confirmed that the GC was modified using the synthesized NB-CNDs. Hydrazine hydrate oxidation response was not observed significantly in 0.1 M PBS solution as shown in Fig. 9a. Fig. 9b shows the cyclic voltammogramic responses for oxidation of HH against the 0.25 μL additions of 200 mM HH (overall concentration of HH in cells was increased from 0 mM to 3 mM). From the graph, it could be observed that the peak current was gradually increased when concentration of HH was increased to 3 mM which indicated the oxidation of HH brought forth by the efficient electrocatalytic action of NB-CNDs.

Fig. 10 depicts the plot between Current Vs Concentration which also supports the same. In which the peak current could be seen increasing linearly against the concentration of HH which revealed that the concentration and current were dependent. In this plot, the linearity was observed for current against its concentration with R2 = 0.98997 (Fig. 10). These results confirmed that the synthesized NB-CNDs act as an efficient electro catalyst for the oxidation of HH.

The mechanism of HH oxidation mainly depends on the nature of electrode and electrolyte. Hydrazine was present in its unprotonated form to a large extent. However a small amount of protonated form also existed in the electrolytic solution. The rate determining step is the one electron transfer followed by a three-electron process to give N2 and H2O as the final product. The overall reaction for the electro oxidation of HH in PBS can be expressed as the following reaction [69].
N 2
H 4
+
4OH −
→
N 2
+
4H 2
O +
4e −
E 0
= 1.61
V

Mostly, Co and Ni are most widely used monometallic catalyst for the oxidation of hydrazine hydrate. In this case, we had used the toxic metal free and eco-friendly green catalyst (NB-CNDs) having significant electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of hydrazine hydrate.

The interesting fluorescent properties of the NB-CNDs made us check whether these NB-CNDs could be employed as probes and are suitable for live cell imaging applications. The fluorescence imaging assay with synthesized NB-CNDs was analyzed through the bio-imaging experiments with HeLa cells. The cells were incubated with NB-CNDs (50 μg/mL) for 4 h. After incubtion, the cells were washed and their fluorescent property was analyzed by confocal fluorescence microscopy. The investigation of cytotoxicity is the basic requirement for applying the synthesized NB-CNDs as fluorescent probe for cell imaging. Hence, the cytotoxicity of the NB-CNDs against HeLa cells was tested using MTT assay. In general, the hetero atom doped CDs are less toxic to cells [37,51,52]. Using minimum concentration (10 μg/mL) of NB-CNDs, the cell viability of HeLa cells was found to be almost 100% (Fig. 11 ). When increasing the concentration from 10 to 50 μg/mL (five different concentrations (i.e. 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μg/mL)), minimum cell death was only caused by NB-CNDs. More than 70% of cell viability could be observed in HeLa cells at 50 μg/mL concentration of NB-CNDs. The results clearly showed less cytotoxicity and biocompatible nature of the synthesized NB-CNDs [37,68].

Moreover, the high aqueous solubility, excellent fluorescent nature and least cytotoxicity make NB-CNDs as the best candidate for the use as fluorescent probes for cell imaging [17,35,49]. The Fig. 12 shows the confocal fluorescence microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with NB-CNDs. The Fig. 12(a) shows the bright field image of HeLa cells. The Fig. 12(b), (c) and (d) are the fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells when excitation at 410 nm (blue), 480 nm (green) and 580 nm (red), respectively. These images clearly indicated the cellular uptakes of the synthesized NB-CNDs and fluorescence microscopy efficiently captured the blue, green and red fluorescent images of HeLa cells at their corresponding excitations. Thus, these results revealed that the synthesized NB-CNDs could penetrate efficiently into the cell walls of the HeLa cells and diffused uniformly. From these results, it is evident that the synthesized NB-CNDs could be profitably utilized as proficient alternate staining agent for bio-imaging applications [60,70].

NB-CNDs were successfully synthesized from fruit extract of S. betaceum, by simple and efficient hydrothermal carbonization techniques. The surface morphology and size of the NB-CNDs were determined using HR-TEM. The shape of the synthesized nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots were found to be spherical and the average size was 5 nm in diameter. XRD and Raman spectrum confirmed the graphitic nature of the NB-CNDs. The doping of N and B and their functional groups present in the surface of CDs were confirmed with the help of FT-IR, EDS and XPS spectroscopic studies. The excellent electrocatalytic ability of the synthesized NB-CNDs was successfully investigated towards the oxidation of hydrazine hydrate using cyclic voltammetry. The fluorescent properties of the NB-CNDs were successfully exploited to use these NB-CNDs as fluorescent staining agent in the bio-imaging of the HeLa cells.

The experimental section including materials and chemicals, preparation of S. betaceum fruit extract, synthesis of NB-CNDs, characterization methods, electrocatalytic activity of synthesized NB-CNDs, cytotoxicity study using MTT assay, bio-imaging study and Quantum yield calculation for this work are supplied in Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM).
Individual contribution of the authors are given below.
Dr. V. Arul: Conceptualization; Data curation; Methodology Investigation; Visualization; Roles/Writing - original draft;
Mr. P. Chandrasekaran: Funding acquisition, Validation; Resources; Formal analysis; Methodology; Visualization.
Dr. G. Sivaraman: Methodology Investigation; Formal analysis.
Dr. M. G. Sethuraman: Manuscript overall correction, Project administration; Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Author declares that there are no conflicts of interest for this work.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors thank the DST–Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB) for the financial support for this work through a major research project on “Eco-friendly ............Catalysis” (Sanction No: EMR/2016/006024). The authors also place on record the encouragement from the authorities of GRI.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108437.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications
Green Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu ML, Chen BB, Li CM, Huang CZ. Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications. Green Chem. 2019;21(3):449-71

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitized carbon dots emitting strong green photoluminescence
J. Mater. Chem. C
